metadata.summary: Backend testing patterns for Ruby/RSpec, PHP/Laravel+Pest, Java/Spring Boot, C#/.NET xUnit, and Rust — canonical idioms and LLM gotchas per language.

content.title: Backend Language Testing: Ruby, PHP, Java, C#, Rust
content.summary: Each backend language has a canonical test idiom. This methodology provides the minimum pattern for Ruby/RSpec, PHP/Laravel+Pest, Java/Spring Boot, C#/.NET xUnit, and Rust, plus the LLM gotchas specific to each stack so agents produce idiomatic tests on the first pass.

why.p: Each language section is a single canonical example, not a decision matrix. Without a canonical pattern, agents pick the wrong framework (RSpec vs Minitest, Pest vs PHPUnit, JUnit 4 vs JUnit 5) and produce tests that fight the project standard. Agents also tend to over-use full container boot (Spring's @SpringBootTest, .NET's WebApplicationFactory) even for unit-level service tests, blowing test time.
Providing the correct idiom per language reduces the review burden from "fix the framework choice" to "add edge cases".

list.item: Bootstrapping a test suite in a new Ruby on Rails, PHP Laravel, Java Spring, C# .NET, or Rust service.
Filling coverage holes on an existing backend module — feed source + this reference and ask for unit/integration tests in the project idiom.
Standardising test style across a polyglot backend monorepo where each service uses a different stack.
Producing first-cut tests during SDD in-progress/ so review focuses on logic, not boilerplate.
Python/Django — see testing-django-pytest for the pytest + factory_boy idiom.
Frontend JS/TS — see testing-js-ts-frontend for vitest + Testing Library.
Performance / load testing — see perf-test-basics, perf-test-tools.
When the project already has a strong test convention — agent will drift into the generic style here and create churn.

section.name: ruby-rspec
section.p: Ruby on Rails projects default to RSpec for unit and service tests. The canonical pattern uses describe/context/let and expects results to be_success or satisfy domain assertions. Use factory_bot for object creation (not fixtures).

rule.statement: Ruby tests MUST use RSpec (not Minitest) with let blocks for test data and factory_bot factories for object graphs. Tests MUST be free of slow I/O unless explicitly labelled :integration.
rule.rationale: RSpec's expressive DSL and factory_bot are the Rails community standard for service-layer tests. Minitest is valid for simpler unit tests but agents should default to RSpec unless the Gemfile shows minitest as the project runner.

example.title: RSpec service test
example.note: described_class avoids hardcoding the class name; let is lazily evaluated. Separate example per concept.

antipattern.description: Slow, flaky tests from missing let! vs let distinction — let is lazy; let! forces evaluation immediately in before blocks.
antipattern.reason: Test ordering sensitivity emerges when objects created lazily are mutated by other examples. Use let! for objects that must exist before every example in the group.

section.name: php-laravel
section.p: Laravel projects should use Pest (the default since Laravel 11) over raw PHPUnit. Pest's function syntax is lighter. Use the RefreshDatabase trait to isolate test DB state.

rule.statement: Laravel tests MUST use Pest function syntax with RefreshDatabase for any test touching the database. HTTP endpoint tests MUST use Laravel's $this->postJson / getJson assertion chain rather than raw Guzzle or curl.
rule.rationale: Pest is the Laravel default and produces cleaner output than PHPUnit class syntax. RefreshDatabase wraps each test in a transaction to avoid state leak. The built-in JSON assertion methods check status and structure in one call.

example.title: Laravel Pest HTTP test
example.note: Pest function style. assertStatus + assertJson chained — no raw PHPUnit assertions needed.

example.title: Raw PHPUnit class in a Pest project
example.note: PHPUnit class style works but agents should use Pest function syntax in Laravel 11+ projects.

section.name: java-spring-boot
section.p: Spring Boot tests default to JUnit 5 + AssertJ. The critical rule: prefer test slices (@DataJpaTest, @WebMvcTest, @ServiceTest) over full context (@SpringBootTest) except for integration tests. Full context boots the entire application — adds seconds per test class.

rule.statement: Spring Boot unit and slice tests MUST NOT use @SpringBootTest unless the test genuinely requires the full application context. Service tests MUST use plain unit tests with Mockito. Repository tests MUST use @DataJpaTest. Controller tests MUST use @WebMvcTest.
rule.rationale: @SpringBootTest adds 5-30 seconds of context startup to every test class that uses it. Test slices boot only the relevant layers; controller slices are 10x faster than full context for endpoint tests.

example.title: Spring service test with Mockito (no full context)
example.note: MockitoExtension, no Spring context, sub-millisecond startup. Use AssertJ assertThat for readable assertions.

example.title: @SpringBootTest for a service unit test
example.note: @SpringBootTest boots the entire application for a service unit test — unnecessary 10-30 second overhead per class.

antipattern.description: Agent adds @SpringBootTest to all test classes because the example in documentation uses it.
antipattern.reason: Force @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class) for services, @DataJpaTest for repositories, @WebMvcTest for controllers in the prompt. Only use @SpringBootTest for explicit integration tests labelled @Tag("integration").

section.name: csharp-dotnet
section.p: .NET projects use xUnit as the default (NUnit is legacy). Dependency injection testing uses Moq or NSubstitute for mocks. FluentAssertions provides readable assertion messages.

rule.statement: .NET service tests MUST use xUnit [Fact] / [Theory] with Moq (or NSubstitute) for dependencies and FluentAssertions for assertions. Tests MUST NOT use WebApplicationFactory unless testing HTTP middleware or integration scenarios.
rule.rationale: xUnit is the .NET standard. FluentAssertions error messages name the expected and actual values clearly, reducing debug time. WebApplicationFactory boots the full ASP.NET pipeline — reserve it for integration tests.

example.title: xUnit service test with Moq
example.note: Constructor injection used directly — no DI container in unit tests. FluentAssertions .Should().Be() for readable assertion.

antipattern.description: Agent uses new Mock without setting up the method under test, leaving it returning null/default.
antipattern.reason: Untriggered mocks return default values silently; the test passes vacuously. Always verify mock setup covers every call the SUT makes.

section.name: rust-testing
section.p: Rust has first-class test support in cargo. Unit tests live in a #[cfg(test)] mod tests module in the same file. Integration tests live in tests/. Async tests require the #[tokio::test] attribute from tokio.

rule.statement: Rust unit tests MUST use #[test] in a #[cfg(test)] mod tests block in the same file as the implementation. Async tests MUST use #[tokio::test] with the tokio runtime — never mix #[test] with async fn without a runtime wrapper. Integration tests MUST live in tests/ and MUST NOT use #[cfg(test)].
rule.rationale: Mixing #[test] and async fn without a runtime wrapper compiles but panics at runtime with "no runtime found". The #[cfg(test)] guard ensures test code is never compiled into production binaries.

example.title: Rust unit and async test
example.note: Sync test uses #[test]; async test uses #[tokio::test]. Both inside #[cfg(test)] mod tests.

antipattern.description: Agent mixes #[test] with async fn, producing a function that compiles but panics at runtime.
antipattern.reason: async fn under #[test] (without tokio::test) creates a future that is never polled — the test body never runs, assertion is never reached, test "passes". Explicitly require #[tokio::test] for all async test functions in the prompt.

list.item: Ruby: bundle exec rspec — run suite; rspec spec/services/ — run service specs only
PHP: php artisan test (Laravel); ./vendor/bin/pest --coverage
Java: mvn test or gradle test; mvn test -pl module-name for monorepo slices
.NET: dotnet test; dotnet test --filter Category=Unit
Rust: cargo test; cargo test --lib for unit only; cargo test --test integration_test
Rust coverage: cargo tarpaulin --out Html (install: cargo install cargo-tarpaulin)
Ruby mutation: mutant (bundle add mutant); PHP mutation: infection/infection
One assertion concept per test — name the test after the concept, not a sequence number.
Use factories / builders for object graphs — raw ORM / constructor calls rot with model changes.
Speed budget: unit under 50 ms, integration under 1 s, full unit suite under 60 s.
Fail loud on flaky tests: quarantine with a tag/marker, never add retry decorators that hide race conditions.
Forbid network in unit tests — use mocks/stubs; agents otherwise add live API calls.
Coverage as a goal, not a target: require mutation score on critical modules, not just line coverage.

section.name: services-and-tools
section.p: Testing infrastructure services useful across all backend stacks:

list.item: Codecov (SaaS) — coverage diff on PRs; agents can read the Codecov API to gate merges.
Coveralls (SaaS) — free tier for OSS; same niche as Codecov.
SonarQube / SonarCloud (SaaS + OSS) — quality gate on coverage + smells; REST API.
Testcontainers (OSS) — real DB/Redis/Kafka in tests; available for Java, .NET, Python, Go, Rust.
Wiremock / MockServer (OSS) — HTTP fakes for integration tests across all languages.
GitHub Actions (SaaS) — cache test runners, shard via matrices for parallel test execution.
